mnemonics: Diamond DILEMMA: Directly Inheriting Leads to Explicit Member Ambiguity
AMBIGUOUS ANCESTORS: Always Mention Base Class (using ::) to Overcome
V.I.P. (Virtual Inheritance Prevents) Duplicate Inheritance Problems
keyPoints: A class can inherit from more than one base class simultaneously, combining their members.
Ambiguity arises when multiple base classes have members with identical names; resolved using the scope resolution operator (::).
The 'diamond problem' occurs when a class inherits from two classes that both inherit from a common base; solved using virtual inheritance.
commonMistakes: Not resolving ambiguous member access, leading to compilation errors.
Forgetting to use virtual inheritance when designing a diamond hierarchy, resulting in duplicate subobjects and data.
Incorrectly assuming the order of constructor calls for multiple base classes (they are called in the order of declaration, not the order in the inheritance list).
